concepts.title: Why gender & agreement are the foundation of Portuguese
concepts.simpleExplanation: Every Portuguese noun is masculine or feminine, and the little word in front shows which: o livro, a casa, um carro, uma mesa. Nouns ending in -o are usually masculine and -a usually feminine, but there are enough surprises (o dia, a mão) that the safe habit is to learn each noun WITH its article. Gender then ripples outward: adjectives and articles must agree in gender AND number, uma casa pequena, uns carros pequenos. Get the article right and the whole sentence dresses itself correctly.
concepts.analogy: Gender is like a dress code that one word sets for the whole party: the noun picks the colour (masculine or feminine), and every word that accompanies it (article, adjective) has to show up wearing the same colour.

rubricPoints.point: Every noun has a gender shown by its article
rubricPoints.reTeach: o/um = masculine, a/uma = feminine; -o is usually masculine and -a feminine, but learn exceptions like o dia, a mão.

rubricPoints.point: Learn the noun WITH its article
rubricPoints.reTeach: Store 'a casa', never bare 'casa', the article is part of the word.

rubricPoints.point: Adjectives agree in gender and number and usually follow the noun
rubricPoints.reTeach: um carro pequeno, uma casa pequena, umas casas pequenas, the endings must match.

rubricPoints.point: Agreement also feeds the contractions
rubricPoints.reTeach: You can only pick do/da or no/na correctly if you know the noun's gender: vou ao mercado, moro na cidade.

concepts.title: Vowel reduction: why Portugal 'swallows' its vowels
concepts.simpleExplanation: European Portuguese keeps only the STRESSED vowel of a word at full volume; the unstressed ones shrink. Unstressed e nearly vanishes (telefone sounds like 't'lefón''), unstressed o becomes 'u' (gato = 'gátu'), and unstressed a becomes a short 'uh'. This single habit explains why Portugal sounds fast and 'swallowed' compared with Spanish or Brazilian Portuguese, and why native speech seems to be missing syllables you can see on the page. It works in your favour too: to sound European, find the stressed syllable, say it clearly, and let everything else collapse.
concepts.analogy: A word is like a photo with one subject in focus: the stressed vowel is sharp in the foreground, and all the unstressed vowels are the blurred background, still there, but deliberately faded so the subject pops.

rubricPoints.point: Unstressed e nearly disappears
rubricPoints.reTeach: telefone -> 't'lefón'', pequeno -> 'p'kénu', the written e is a whisper or gone.

rubricPoints.point: Unstressed o = 'u', unstressed a = short 'uh'
rubricPoints.reTeach: obrigado ends in 'u'; casa ends in a short closed 'uh', never a full 'ah'.

rubricPoints.point: Only the stressed vowel keeps full colour
rubricPoints.reTeach: Find the stress (usually second-to-last syllable, or where the accent mark is) and give only that vowel full value.

rubricPoints.point: This is the key to understanding fast native speech
rubricPoints.reTeach: When Portuguese sounds like it's missing syllables, it is, mentally restore the reduced e's and o's to match the written word.

concepts.title: ser vs estar: the two verbs 'to be'
concepts.simpleExplanation: Portuguese splits English 'to be' into two verbs. Ser is for what something IS by nature: identity, origin, profession, permanent traits, telling the time (sou português, ela é médica, são três horas). Estar is for how something is RIGHT NOW: location, temporary states, moods, the weather (estou em casa, estás cansado, está frio). The same sentence changes meaning with the swap: o café é bom says the coffee is good as a product; o café está bom says this particular cup tastes good today. Estar also builds the European progressive: estou a trabalhar (I am working).
concepts.analogy: Ser is the ID card (facts printed once that describe who you are; estar is the status light on your phone) green now, busy in an hour. 'Sou português' is on the card; 'estou cansado' is today's status.

rubricPoints.point: Ser = essence: identity, origin, profession, traits, time
rubricPoints.reTeach: Sou de Lisboa, ela é médica, o mar é azul, são três horas.

rubricPoints.point: Estar = current state: location, mood, weather, ongoing action
rubricPoints.reTeach: Estou em casa, estás triste?, está frio, está a chover.

rubricPoints.point: The swap changes meaning
rubricPoints.reTeach: A sopa é boa (it's a good dish) vs a sopa está fria (it's cold right now).

rubricPoints.point: Both are irregular and must be memorised first
rubricPoints.reTeach: sou/és/é/somos/são and estou/estás/está/estamos/estão.

concepts.title: PPS vs imperfeito: photo vs film
concepts.simpleExplanation: Portuguese tells the past two ways. The pretérito perfeito simples (PPS) reports a completed event, what happened: falei, fui, fiz. The imperfeito paints the background, what was going on: habits (morava em Braga = I used to live in Braga), descriptions, scene-setting, age and time in the past (era pequeno, eram oito horas). They combine in every story: Estava a chover (background) quando saí (event). Bonus: the PPS also covers the English present perfect, 'I have visited Porto' is simply visitei o Porto, and the imperfeito doubles as everyday politeness: queria um café (I'd like a coffee).
concepts.analogy: The imperfeito is the film running in the background, rain falling, people being young, habits repeating. The PPS is the photo: a single click that freezes one completed event on top of that film. Stories are photos pinned onto film.

rubricPoints.point: PPS = completed event, the 'photo'
rubricPoints.reTeach: falei, comi, fui, fiz, one finished action: Ontem falámos com a vizinha.

rubricPoints.point: Imperfeito = background, habit, description, the 'film'
rubricPoints.reTeach: -ava/-ia endings: morava, comia; irregulars era, tinha, ia, vinha. Quando era pequeno, morava no Algarve.

rubricPoints.point: They combine in narration
rubricPoints.reTeach: Estava a ler (scene) quando o telefone tocou (event).

rubricPoints.point: PPS also covers the English present perfect; imperfeito is polite
rubricPoints.reTeach: 'I have eaten' = já comi (never a compound tense); queria um café = I'd like a coffee.

concepts.title: Ênclise vs próclise: where the little pronouns go
concepts.simpleExplanation: In European Portuguese, object and reflexive pronouns (me, te, se, o, lhe...) attach AFTER the verb with a hyphen by default, this is ênclise: chamo-me Rui, vejo-a, levanto-me cedo. But certain 'magnet' words pull the pronoun to BEFORE the verb (próclise): negation (não me chamo), question words (como te chamas?), que-clauses (acho que se chama Ana), and adverbs like já, também, ainda, nunca, só. There are also two sound rules: after -r/-s/-z the pronoun o/a becomes lo/la and the consonant drops (comprar + o = comprá-lo), and after a nasal ending it becomes no/na (fizeram-no). Brazil defaults to pronoun-before-verb; the hyphenated default is a badge of European Portuguese.
concepts.analogy: The pronoun is a trailer hitched behind the verb, that's its normal parking spot (chamo-me). But words like não, que and já are powerful magnets standing before the verb: when one is present, it snaps the trailer off the back and pulls it in front (não me chamo).

rubricPoints.point: Default = ênclise: pronoun after the verb with a hyphen
rubricPoints.reTeach: Chamo-me Sofia. Levanto-me às sete. Ela senta-se ali.

rubricPoints.point: Triggers flip it to próclise: não, question words, que, já/também/ainda/nunca/só
rubricPoints.reTeach: Não me chamo Pedro. Como te chamas? Acho que se chama Ana. Já me lembro!

rubricPoints.point: Sound rules: -lo/-la after -r/-s/-z, -no/-na after nasals
rubricPoints.reTeach: comprar + o = vou comprá-lo; fazer + a = vou fazê-la; fizeram + o = fizeram-no.

rubricPoints.point: This placement is a European signature
rubricPoints.reTeach: Portugal says chamo-me; if you put the pronoun first with no trigger, it sounds Brazilian.

concepts.title: The conjuntivo: the mood of wishes and maybes
concepts.simpleExplanation: The indicative states facts; the conjuntivo (subjunctive) handles everything that is NOT yet fact, wishes (quero que venhas), necessity (é preciso que estejas), doubt (duvido que ele queira), purpose (para que saibas), concession (embora seja caro) and talvez. The present conjuntivo is built from the eu-form of the present: drop -o and swap the vowel, -ar verbs take -e (falo → fale), -er/-ir verbs take -a (como → coma). Because it starts from the eu-form, irregular stems come free: faço → faça, tenho → tenha, digo → diga. Only seven verbs are truly irregular: seja, esteja, vá, dê, saiba, queira, haja.
concepts.analogy: The indicative is a news report, it tells you what IS. The conjuntivo is a letter to Father Christmas, it lists what you want, fear, doubt or hope might happen. Same events, but one describes the world and the other describes your wishes about it.

rubricPoints.point: Used for wish, necessity, doubt, purpose, concession, not facts
rubricPoints.reTeach: querer que, é preciso que, duvidar que, para que, embora, talvez all take the conjuntivo.

rubricPoints.point: Formed from the eu-form with the 'opposite' vowel
rubricPoints.reTeach: falo -> fale, como -> coma, parto -> parta; irregular stems carry over: faço -> faça, tenho -> tenha.

rubricPoints.point: Seven true irregulars
rubricPoints.reTeach: seja, esteja, vá, dê, saiba, queira, haja.

rubricPoints.point: It hides inside everyday commands
rubricPoints.reTeach: Formal and negative imperatives ARE conjuntivo forms: fale!, não fales!, não te preocupes.

concepts.title: Tenho estudado is NOT 'I have studied'
concepts.simpleExplanation: The pretérito perfeito composto (ter + participle) looks exactly like the English present perfect but means something much narrower: repetition or continuous duration from the recent past up to now. Tenho estudado muito = 'I have been studying a lot LATELY', never 'I studied once'. For a single completed event, even one connected to the present, Portuguese uses the simple past: 'I have visited Lisbon' is visitei Lisboa. The test: if you can add 'lately' or 'repeatedly' to the English sentence, the composto fits; otherwise use the PPS. It loves companions like ultimamente, nos últimos tempos, este mês.
concepts.analogy: English 'I have done' is a single stamp in your passport, one event, still relevant. Tenho feito is a gym attendance card getting punched again and again in recent weeks. If your sentence is one stamp, use the simple past (fiz); only a card full of fresh punches earns tenho feito.

rubricPoints.point: PPC = repetition/duration up to now, not one event
rubricPoints.reTeach: Tenho dormido mal = I have been sleeping badly (lately); tem chovido muito = it has been raining a lot.

rubricPoints.point: Single completed events take the PPS, even with English 'have'
rubricPoints.reTeach: 'I have visited Porto' = visitei o Porto; 'Have you eaten?' = já comeste?

rubricPoints.point: The 'lately/repeatedly' test
rubricPoints.reTeach: If the English works with 'lately' or 'again and again', use tenho + participle; if not, use the simple past.

rubricPoints.point: Typical time companions
rubricPoints.reTeach: ultimamente, nos últimos tempos, este mês, nas últimas semanas.

concepts.title: How the CAPLE exams work
concepts.simpleExplanation: Portugal's official exam system is CAPLE, run by the University of Lisbon under the Camões institute. The ladder climbs the CEFR: ACESSO (A1), CIPLE (A2, the certificate accepted for Portuguese nationality), DEPLE (B1, your milestone), DIPLE (B2, the job/academic target), then DAPLE and DUPLE (C1/C2). DEPLE and DIPLE have four components: reading comprehension, written production and interaction, listening comprehension, and oral production and interaction (CIPLE merges things into three). The result is a single global percentage across components with a named grade: Suficiente (55-69%) is the PASS, Bom is 70-84%, Muito Bom is 85-100%, and below 55% fails. Unlike the French DELF, there is no published per-section minimum, but a weak section drags the only number that counts.
concepts.analogy: The CAPLE grade is one big soup pot: every section pours into the same pot and only the final blend is tasted, it must reach 55% to pass. The DELF, by contrast, tastes each ingredient separately and can fail you on one bad ingredient. In the CAPLE pot a weak section can hide... but only if the others are strong enough to lift the average.

rubricPoints.point: The exam ladder: ACESSO A1, CIPLE A2, DEPLE B1, DIPLE B2, DAPLE/DUPLE C1/C2
rubricPoints.reTeach: CIPLE is also the level accepted for Portuguese nationality; DEPLE is the B1 milestone, DIPLE the B2 target.

rubricPoints.point: DEPLE/DIPLE have four components
rubricPoints.reTeach: Reading, writing (production & interaction), listening, and speaking; Corlang weighs them equally at 25% each.

rubricPoints.point: Pass rule: global average ≥ 55% = Suficiente
rubricPoints.reTeach: Suficiente 55-69, Bom 70-84, Muito Bom 85-100, below 55 fails; one single percentage, no published per-section floor.

rubricPoints.point: The app mirrors this
rubricPoints.reTeach: Corlang's mock exams average the four sections and apply the real ≥ 55% Suficiente rule.
